title: Changing how atrial fibrillation is treated
text:

Atrial fibrillation can cause blood clots — as seen in this illustration — to form in the heart, which can lead to strokes.Credit: Sciepro/Science Photo Library/GettyAt this moment, electrical pulses are sweeping through the chambers of your heart, inducing them to beat in regular pulses. In atrial fibrillation, however, those signals go rogue and cause the quivering of the heart in fast flutters. This quivering increases the risk of stroke fivefold.While ageing is a major contributor to atrial fibrillation, other conditions of older age may limit treatment options. Over the past few years, however, US-based medical-device company Boston Scientific has been exploring next-generation medical devices that it hopes can help overcome major barriers to treatment, expanding the options available for patients, including elderly people.In atrial fibrillation, the heart struggles to pump blood as it should. The quivering upper chambers and irregular heartbeat cause blood to stagnate in parts of the heart. This stagnation can result in the formation of blood clots, which can make their way out of the heart and cause a stroke.Treatment evolutionStrokes associated with atrial fibrillation tend to be more serious than other strokes, notes Michifumi Tokuda, a cardiologist, and an electrophysiologist at Jikei University Hospital in Tokyo, Japan, who has clinical experience using Boston Scientific devices.Recent evidence also suggests that atrial fibrillation may contribute to the development of heart failure by weakening the organ over time. In Japan, the risk of hospitalization from heart failure increases 18-fold for those with atrial fibrillation1.A catheter used to deliver radio-frequency pulses to the heart.Credit: romaset/iStock/GettyAtrial fibrillation is an area in cardiology where technology for treatment has arguably progressed the most, says Kazuhiro Satomi, a cardiologist and electrophysiologist at Tokyo Medical University, who also has clinical experience using Boston Scientific devices.Treatment was transformed nearly two decades ago with the wider adoption of ablation for atrial fibrillation. With developments in 3D mapping, physicians were able to navigate catheters to target tissue — most often the pulmonary veins, which connect the lungs and heart — and burn or freeze them to eliminate electric signals.In rare cases during thermal ablation, excessive heat or cold can travel beyond the heart tissue and severely damage nearby organs such as the lungs, oesophagus and important nerves. For example, thermal ablation could create a fistula between the upper heart chambers and the esophagus — which has a high rate of mortality1 — as well as narrowing of the pulmonary veins and causing permanent nerve damage.As these complications are more life-threatening in people 75 and older, who were often deemed poor candidates for ablation in Japan.Next-generation toolsThe latest catheter instruments, including those developed by Boston Scientific, are helping reduce the risks of complications. Called pulsed-field ablation (PFA), the technology relies on tissue-selective, non-thermal electric fields to ablate heart tissue and avoid damage to surrounding structures.With this, patients well into their 80s and 90s have been able to receive treatments for atrial fibrillation, says Satomi.“We’re not sure yet whether it prolongs lifespan,” he adds. “But the technology is helping patients lead self-sufficient lives for longer.”Pulsed-field ablation is more selective than thermal ablation and so reduces the risk of damaging structures near the heart.Credit: magicmine/iStock/GettyBoston Scientific’s catheter device was approved in Japan in 2024, and now many hospitals use the device as their main treatment tool for atrial fibrillation, says Satomi. The device has also been approved for use in the United States, United Kingdom and EU.“PFA has transformed clinical practice,” he says, noting that in some cases physicians have been able to halve operation times. That has eased the burden on patients and healthcare providers alike. “It’s the first time I’ve seen a technology be adopted so quickly across the board.”While traditional ablation was a skill that took years to master, PFA has made the procedure more accessible, Tokuda says. “More facilities, including those in smaller cities, can now offer ablation at a consistent standard,” he explains.“However, PFA isn’t universal yet,” Tokuda adds. “Most current devices are designed for the pulmonary veins, where most faulty signals originate. Further innovation is still needed to expand treatment options beyond the pulmonary veins.”Alternative strategiesDifferent devices developed by Boston Scientific and other companies have opened the doors to new treatment strategies. Left atrial appendage closure (LAAC) devices are designed to reduce the risk of stroke due to blood clots forming in the heart.The left atrial appendage is a small chamber where about 90% of blood clots originating from the heart develop. Once LAAC devices are inserted in the left atrial appendage, they open like an umbrella to seal off the chamber.These devices serve as a potential alternative to blood thinners for atrial-fibrillation patients who have a high risk of bleeding. Patients with high stroke risk are often prescribed anticoagulants for life. However, particularly in older patients, anticoagulants can increase the risk of dangerous bleeding, heightened by the likelihood of falling. In some patients, when the risk of bleeding is judged to outweigh stroke-prevention benefit, long-term anticoagulation may no longer be appropriate.In other cases, patients experience strokes despite taking anticoagulants, or after receiving ablation. “LAAC devices open up more options for difficult-to-treat patients,” says Satomi.Remaining challengesA key remaining challenge is the treatment of so-called persistent atrial fibrillation, says Tokuda. In some patients, atrial fibrillation spreads further into the atrium after pulmonary vein ablation. When this happens, episodes become longer and symptoms such as shortness of breath and fatigue worsen.“We still don’t have a good idea of what the best course of treatment should look like for persistent atrial fibrillation,” he says.AI and other emerging mapping approaches may help identify additional faulty signals sources in individual patients. Those receiving AI-guided ablation were more likely to stay free of atrial fibrillation over the long term2. These procedures also took longer, however, and tools such as PFA may boost efficiency and effectiveness.Satomi hopes to see progress in early diagnosis. For example, some patients have ‘hidden’ atrial fibrillation in which the spasms come and go. Here, electrocardiogram readings appear normal. Others are asymptomatic. “It’s not uncommon for patients to be diagnosed only after a stroke,” he says.Other devices such as smartwatches and implantable loop recorders could advance early diagnosis through continuous monitoring. They also provide insights into atrial fibrillation’s relationship with lifestyle habits such as alcohol consumption and exercise, as well as with conditions including sleep apnoea, hypertension, obesity and diabetes. The accumulation of such real-world data is making it possible to study risk factors in unprecedented detail.“The way we think about treating atrial fibrillation is changing significantly,” says Tokuda. “Where ablation was once the sole treatment, there is now increased focus on addressing lifestyle choices and other related diseases after ablation.”
Learn more about atrial fibrillation and the latest Boston Scientific devices for treating it.
